terms.def: An optimisation where the objective and feasible region have no local optima; guaranteed global solution via solvers.
Minimise a quadratic objective (e.g. w′Σw) subject to linear constraints; the standard MVO problem class.
Python convex optimisation library; expressive domain-specific language for formulating and solving portfolio problems.
Add λ||w||₁ or λ||w||₂² to the objective; promotes sparsity (L1) or diversification (L2) in portfolio weights.
||w_new − w_old||₁ ≤ T; limits transaction costs by restricting how much the portfolio changes each rebalance.
Every convex primal problem has a dual; dual variables are shadow prices on constraints (e.g. cost of tightening a weight limit).

eli3: Portfolio optimisation is a mathematical problem: choose weights for a set of assets to maximise expected return while controlling risk. Convex optimisation is the mathematical framework for solving these problems efficiently and reliably. A problem is 'convex' if the objective and constraints have a specific mathematical shape that guarantees we can find the globally best solution — no local optima to get trapped in.

Most portfolio problems are convex: minimise variance subject to a return target; maximise Sharpe ratio; minimise tracking error — all convex. This means solvers can find the exact optimal solution in milliseconds, even for portfolios with thousands of assets and hundreds of constraints. The quality of the solution depends entirely on the quality of the inputs (expected returns, covariance matrix).

Practitioners use convex optimisation libraries (CVXPY, Gurobi, quadprog) to solve portfolio problems with realistic constraints: position limits, sector weights, turnover limits, factor exposure targets, ESG scores, tax lots. The constraint set defines the feasible portfolio space; optimisation finds the best point within it.

whenWhy.title: Mean-variance optimisation: the core problem
whenWhy.body: Standard MVO: min_w w'Σw - λ w'μ s.t. Σw_i = 1, w_i ≥ 0 (long-only). This is a quadratic program (QP): quadratic objective, linear constraints — convex. Efficient frontier: trace by varying λ from 0 (minimum variance) to ∞ (maximum expected return). For N = 500 assets: solved in milliseconds using interior-point methods (OSQP, Gurobi). Adding constraints: (1) Long-short: allow w_i < 0; (2) Position limits: -u_i ≤ w_i ≤ u_i (box constraints); (3) Sector constraints: Σ_{i∈sector} w_i ≤ s_max; (4) Turnover: |w_i - w_{i,prev}| ≤ τ_i; (5) Net leverage: Σ|w_i| ≤ L_max; (6) Beta target: w'β = β_target; (7) Factor exposure: Σ_i w_i × factor_exposure_ij = target_j. All these are linear constraints — the QP remains convex with them.

whenWhy.title: Transaction cost-aware optimisation
whenWhy.body: Transaction cost penalty: add a cost term to the objective. For round-trip cost model: penalise |w_i - w_{i,prev}| × tc_i. But absolute value is not differentiable — use auxiliary variables: introduce t_i ≥ 0 such that t_i ≥ w_i - w_{i,prev} and t_i ≥ w_{i,prev} - w_i (t_i = |Δw_i|). Objective with transaction costs: min_w w'Σw - λ w'μ + κ Σ_i tc_i × t_i. This remains a QP (linear tc penalty). Market impact penalty: for large trades, impact scales as O(√(trade_size/ADV)). This is a non-linear cost — but it's convex (square root of portfolio weights). CVXPY handles such cone constraints. The optimal solution naturally avoids large positions in illiquid stocks because the impact cost outweighs the signal benefit.

whenWhy.title: L1 regularisation and sparse portfolios
whenWhy.body: LASSO regularisation in portfolio optimisation: add λ_L1 Σ|w_i| to the objective (L1 penalty on weights). Effect: drives small positions to exactly zero — creates sparse portfolios. Equivalent to a prior that most weights should be zero; appropriate when the true optimal portfolio is concentrated. Contrast with L2 (Ridge) regularisation (λ_L2 Σw²_i): shrinks all weights toward zero proportionally — creates well-diversified, spread portfolios. Choice: L1 for concentrated signal (few strong views); L2 for diversified signal (many weak views). Elastic net: α L1 + (1-α) L2 combination — sparse but also controls correlation between selected assets. CVXPY example: cp.Problem(cp.Minimize(cp.quad_form(w, Sigma) - lambda_*mu.T @ w + l1_penalty * cp.norm1(w))). Note: with LASSO, the problem is an SOCP (second-order cone program) if the portfolio also has a quadratic risk term; still convex and solvable in milliseconds.

deepDiveIntro: At the optimal portfolio, a set of mathematical conditions (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions) must hold: the gradient of the objective is balanced by the gradients of the binding constraints. Each constraint has an associated multiplier called a shadow price, which tells you how much the objective would improve if that constraint were relaxed by one unit. Constraints that are not binding have a shadow price of zero. Understanding which constraints are binding — and how expensive each one is — gives portfolio managers insight into what is limiting further optimisation.

deepDive.heading: CVXPY and Modern Portfolio Optimisation Libraries
deepDive.body: CVXPY is an open-source Python library that lets you write portfolio optimisation problems in near-mathematical notation. It automatically verifies that your problem is convex (so a global optimum is guaranteed), selects an appropriate solver, and runs it — typically in milliseconds for a few hundred assets. For problems with thousands of stocks, you can exploit the fact that a covariance matrix from a factor model has special structure to reduce computation dramatically from scaling with the square of the number of assets to scaling with the number of factors. [1]

deepDive.heading: Integer and Mixed-Integer Portfolio Problems
deepDive.body: Some constraints require all-or-nothing decisions: either hold at least a minimum position size or hold nothing, hold exactly 50 stocks, or exclude certain securities entirely. These discrete choices break convexity and make the problem combinatorially hard. Commercial solvers like Gurobi and CPLEX handle these mixed-integer problems using branch-and-bound, which is exponential in the worst case but fast in practice for typical portfolio problems. The most common practical workaround is to solve the continuous convex problem first, then threshold small positions to zero — this produces near-optimal results without the computational overhead of integer programming. [2]

deepDive.heading: Constraint Attribution and Portfolio Decomposition
deepDive.body: After optimisation, the shadow prices on binding constraints tell you exactly how costly each restriction is in terms of expected return foregone. If the sector cap on technology has a shadow price of 50 basis points, relaxing it by one percentage point would improve expected return by 50 basis points. This lets a portfolio manager have an informed conversation about whether a compliance constraint is worth its cost. Return attribution decomposes total return into contributions from each factor exposure, providing a complete explanation of why the portfolio looks the way it does. [1]

takeaways: Standard MVO is a QP: min w'Σw - λw'μ; constraints are linear; solved in milliseconds by interior-point methods. [1]
Transaction costs: |Δw| penalty via auxiliary variable t_i; market impact is convex √(size/ADV) cone constraint. [1]
L1 → sparse portfolio (LASSO); L2 → diversified (Ridge); elastic net = combination. [1]
CVXPY: DCP framework verifies convexity automatically; parameterised problems enable fast live rebalancing. [1]
Shadow price on binding constraint = expected return cost of tightening that constraint by ε. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does MVO produce extreme concentrated portfolios, and how do you fix it?
edgeCases.a: MVO amplifies estimation errors: if expected returns are estimated with noise (as they always are), the optimiser doubles down on assets with the highest estimated return. If stock A's true expected return is 5% but estimated at 8%, MVO allocates heavily to it — the estimation error is exploited by the optimiser. This is the 'error maximisation' property of MVO. Fixes: (1) Constrain maximum position size (hard constraint: w_i ≤ 5%); simplest and most common; (2) Shrink expected returns toward the mean (James-Stein shrinkage): pull high estimates down, low estimates up; reduces the range of μ inputs; (3) Black-Litterman: use equilibrium returns as prior; don't trust raw estimates; (4) Resampled efficiency: run 1,000 bootstrapped optimisations, average the weights — robust to individual estimation errors; (5) Robust optimisation: minimise worst-case outcome over uncertainty set of μ estimates. In practice: hard position limits + Ledoit-Wolf covariance + Black-Litterman expected returns is the standard combination used by institutional quantitative equity managers.

edgeCases.q: How do tax lot constraints interact with portfolio optimisation?
edgeCases.a: Tax-lot-aware optimisation: each position consists of multiple lots purchased at different prices with different holding periods. Selling a lot triggers a capital gain (long-term: 20% rate; short-term: ordinary income rate ≈ 37%). The decision of which lots to sell is a combinatorial problem. MVO + tax: at each rebalancing, the after-tax expected return of selling position i depends on which lots are sold. For a $100 gain in a position with $50 embedded short-term gain: after-tax gain ≈ $50 (LTCG: $50×80%) + $50×(1-37%) (STCG) = $40 + $31.50 = $71.50. Tax-efficient optimisation: (1) Only sell positions with tax loss harvesting opportunities (sell losers to realise capital losses); (2) When selling for rebalancing, prefer selling lots with lowest embedded gain (tax-optimised lot selection); (3) Delay selling short-term lots until they convert to long-term (1-year holding period). CVXPY + Gurobi handles tax-lot optimisation as a MIQP.

sources.label: Diamond, S. & Boyd, S. (2016) — CVXPY: A Python-Embedded Modeling Language for Convex Optimization, JMLR
Grinold, R.C. & Kahn, R.N. — Active Portfolio Management (2nd ed.), McGraw-Hill
